speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four with Rose Kohn. So you, then, were able to bring your mother from Detroit to St. Louis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your little boy finally recovered? Yeah, he finally recovered. And we stayed in-- [DOORBELL] OK, sorry for the interruption. So your little boy recovered, and then you decided that you would come to Los Angeles. Why Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why Los Angeles? Because the lady we stayed in St Louis with, was from-- was in Los-- lived in Los Angeles. And my little boy, after that pneumonia, he had a cough without the stop, you know, like asthma. Asthmatic cough. And she said, look, the best thing for you-- you don't have anything. If you want to start life, start life in Los Angeles. So I listened to her. And we bought the tickets, and we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: By train.

speaker: interviewee
text: By train-- I came by plane because I was pregnant with the twins. So I came, and Mother came. And we sent-- my ex came first to find us an apartment, which he never did. And we just-- anyway. So I-- I came here to Los Angeles, and then I started-- that's when life began here. I raised my children here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talk about the twins you had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were born May 15, 1951. And everybody was healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were a boy--

speaker: interviewee
text: A boy and a girl, right. A boy and a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Charles and Lenora. And they-- they're are doing OK now. They had hard times-- from parents from a camp, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think something--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- It's a combination. First of all, they did not have a father that paid attention. OK, first of all. And secondly, I think that with our experiences and my mother's experiences-- she was with us-- must have rubbed off a little, kind of. It was hard for them to grow up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that you transmitted to them a fearfulness or a lack of faith in people, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I really don't know, but it could-- I don't know how to put it, but I tried hard. I was a very devoted mother. I mean, nothing was lacking for my kids. But somehow, my Charles, he was on drugs for many, many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were a few kids on drugs whose parents had not survived concentration camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. I know that, too. I know that, too. Yeah. And the kids-- my daughter doesn't want to get married. I mean, you know-- she's wonderful. They're wonderful. I mean, they're wonderful kids. I'm not-- you know, I think they are just great. They're beautiful children. But I wonder. Sometimes we talk about it. You know? Some fear got into them. Not that I know for sure, I'm just thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your experiences, the experiences that you had, what life lesson would you say they left you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lesson in my life? Hmm. Hmm, well. People can be very cruel. It's a cruel world, and people can be very cruel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you think helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Faith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Faith in what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Faith. Faith in God. I believe in doing good. I am happy when I do something good for people. You know? I like to do good because of so many are not doing good. [LAUGHS] So I wanted-- that's what I feel. And it makes me happy when I can help people in any way. And I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you're very involved in lots of good work, aren't  you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, mm-hm. And I'm very proud of my children. I love them dearly. Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that you're, even with it all, that you, yourself, are a happy person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I can survive very many downfalls. I can survive. I have the ability to survive. And yeah, I'm a positive. I'm a positive thinking person now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe some of your faith is in yourself, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, probably. I don't know how to put it, how to explain it. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, let's see some of your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: The pictures, OK. OK, this picture was done in the ghetto. And this is [NON-ENGLISH] Weinberg, a policeman in the ghetto, approximately in 1942 in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Lodz ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Lodz ghetto, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the right or the left as shown?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's on the right. You know. He saved my mother's life, and he was always a terrific, terrific guy, one of the best people ever. And it's just too bad that he vanished, also. OK, this is the picture of General Eisenhower. And I am in it, too, that I'm so proud of. He came to visit the DP camp, Feldafing, in 1945 after our liberation. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which on are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am in the white-- the white dress, the first in between him and me. I don't know how to describe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Him and the person who he's standing next to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Him and the person he's standing next to, right. Uh-huh. And it was quite, quite an excitement at that time, and I'm still excited the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my mother, which I survived the camp together with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name is in Mary [NON-ENGLISH], and her Yiddish name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And she lived till age of 86. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Germany right after the liberation. And she looked pretty good. [LAUGHING] I was so proud of her, and I miss her a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she died in 1986? She died in 1976. 1976.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wedding party?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is our wedding picture. We were married in 1946 in Germany, in Konstanz and Bodensee. My husband stands behind me. So-- well, and that's been many, many years. And you pointed out before that over on the far left in the uniform--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: The uniform is an American citizen. He worked for the [NON-ENGLISH] that he came at that time. And he also attended our wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that he supplied you with the dress and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: With all the good food and clothes they brought us. And they were really kind. They were really nice to us. They really made us feel good and helpful. Very, very helpful. Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my son, Leo. He was born in 1947 in November the 10th. And this was-- I was giving him bath in this little [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHING] How do you say? And he was just a wonderful little boy. This is when you were living with the woman there, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living in Germany. And we were renting one room from a lady that has a whole apartment. And so we were crowded, but we were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my three children. They are the twins, Charles and Leonora, on the left. And Leo is on my right. And they must be about-- the twins must be about two years old, and my boy, Leo, is 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that would make that what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year? This probably '53. '53.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in California?

speaker: interviewee
text: '53, Right. And it's here in California, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is a Bar Mitzvah picture from my oldest son, Leo. And my mother is in it, too. And there's a very happy family here now. Everybody's happy and having lots of fun. And this was-- yeah, he was 13 at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would be like, 1960?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in California?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, here in California, right. Everything here in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my son-- of my son Charlie's wedding. And it was taking place in Los Angeles, May 10, 1995. And here is the family, all my family and also my two friends from New York, and Harry [INAUDIBLE], my friend, too. And my daughter, Leonora, my son, Leo, and his girlfriend, Francine. And we are very happy that he got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife is Karen, and a wonderful, wonderful girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also the picture from Charlie's wedding. Charlie and Karen, and also her parents, Mr. And Mrs. [INAUDIBLE] and Harry and Rose. And this was also 1995. May the 10th was the wonderful wedding. And I'm so proud of them, and they're doing great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.
